summary:
William Shakespeare (1564–1616 AD) was the greatest writer in the English language and arguably the most influential literary figure in world history, whose 37 plays and 154 sonnets have been translated into every major language, performed more frequently than those of any other playwright, and whose influence on English vocabulary, literary form, and the exploration of human psychology is unparalleled. Working in Elizabethan and Jacobean London — a city of approximately 200,000 people, recently transformed by [[event:3834|printing]], Protestant Reformation, and the Renaissance influx of classical learning — Shakespeare was the principal playwright for the Globe Theatre, a shareholder in the theatrical company the Lord Chamberlain's Men (later the King's Men), and a prolific working professional rather than an isolated genius.

Shakespeare's dramatic range encompassed tragedy (Hamlet, King Lear, Macbeth, Othello, Romeo and Juliet), history plays (the Henry IV and Henry V cycles, Richard III, Julius Caesar), and comedies (A Midsummer Night's Dream, Much Ado About Nothing, The Tempest) of extraordinary diversity and depth. His exploration of power, love, jealousy, ambition, mortality, and identity gave later generations an inexhaustible reservoir of character types and situations; the Oxford English Dictionary credits him with inventing or first recording approximately 1,700 words. His plays' persistent ability to speak to political situations — Hamlet's paralysis before unjust authority, Macbeth's descent through ambition, The Tempest's meditation on colonialism — has made them a perpetual mirror for every era. The 'Shakespeare Question' — whether the works were written by the man from Stratford-upon-Avon or someone else — is a persistent fringe debate, overwhelmingly rejected by mainstream scholarship.
